name: Inverse Trigonometric Functions — Finding the Angle
whatIsIt: Ordinary trig takes an angle and gives a ratio (sin 30° = 1/2). INVERSE trig does the opposite: it takes the ratio and gives back the angle. sin⁻¹(1/2) = 30° asks 'which angle has sine 1/2?'. To keep the answer single, each inverse has a PRINCIPAL VALUE range: sin⁻¹ ∈ [−90°, 90°], cos⁻¹ ∈ [0°, 180°], tan⁻¹ ∈ (−90°, 90°).
whyNeeded: Whenever you know a ratio and need the angle — the angle a ramp makes, the direction of a force, the tilt of a satellite dish — inverse trig gives it. It's essential in physics, navigation, engineering and computer graphics.
realLifeUses: Finding the angle of a ramp from its height and length
The launch angle of a projectile from its speeds
The tilt angle for a solar panel or dish

vocabulary.meaning: Functions that give the ANGLE from a known ratio.
The single chosen answer in a fixed range, so the inverse is a proper function.

story.title: The Ladder's Angle
story.text: A ladder leans so that its height up the wall over its length is 1/2. 'What angle does it make with the ground?' asks Farah. Uncle says the sine of the angle is 1/2, so the angle is sin⁻¹(1/2). 'Which angle has sine one-half?' Farah remembers: 30°. 'Inverse trig turned the ratio back into the angle!' Uncle nods.
story.extractedProblem: The sine of a ladder's angle is 1/2. What is the angle?

visual.caption: Inverse trig runs the triangle backwards: give it the ratio (like sine = 1/2) and it hands back the angle (30°).

teachingGallery.title: Ratio in, angle out
teachingGallery.note: Ask 'which angle gives this ratio?' — inside the principal range.

standardMethod.summary: To evaluate an inverse, ask 'which angle in the principal range gives this ratio?' Use known values in reverse (sin 30° = 1/2 ⇒ sin⁻¹(1/2) = 30°).
steps: Read the ratio you're given (like sin⁻¹ of 1/2).
Recall which standard angle produces that ratio.
Pick the answer inside the principal range (sin⁻¹: −90° to 90°; cos⁻¹: 0° to 180°).
Remember sin(sin⁻¹ x) = x — the two undo each other.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reading sin⁻¹ x as 1/sin x (a reciprocal) instead of 'the angle whose sine is x'.
commonMistakes.fix: sin⁻¹ is the INVERSE FUNCTION (gives an angle), not 1/sin. sin⁻¹(1/2) = 30°, not 2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving any angle with the right ratio, ignoring the principal range.
commonMistakes.fix: Choose the answer inside the principal range so the inverse is single-valued (sin⁻¹ between −90° and 90°).

challenge.q: A student writes sin⁻¹(1/2) = 2. What's the fix?

options.label: The angle whose sine is x

questions.answer: The angle whose sine is x
hintLadder: The angle whose sine is x.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up a ramp whose height/length is 1/2 and explain the angle is sin⁻¹(1/2) = 30°. Make clear sin⁻¹ gives the ANGLE (not 1/sin), and that each inverse has a principal-value range.

revisionCard.summary: Inverse trig gives the ANGLE from a ratio: sin⁻¹(1/2) = 30°, tan⁻¹(1) = 45°, cos⁻¹(1) = 0°, sin⁻¹(1) = 90°. Principal ranges keep answers single: sin⁻¹ ∈ [−90°,90°], cos⁻¹ ∈ [0°,180°], tan⁻¹ ∈ (−90°,90°). sin⁻¹ is NOT 1/sin.

rememberIt.hook: Inverse trig ASKS the angle: sin^-1(x) = 'which angle has this sine?'
rememberIt.unpack: sin^-1, cos^-1, tan^-1 undo sin, cos, tan. sin^-1 is NOT 1/sin. Each has a restricted range so the answer is unique.

studentQuestions.a: No! sin^-1 is the INVERSE (which angle gives this sine). 1/sin is cosec. A common trap.
Many angles share the same sine. Restricting the output (e.g. -90 to 90 degrees) makes the answer a single, definite angle.
